_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology and risk groups per Bousquet et al. JACI 2006 (PMID 16815153). Spina bifida and healthcare worker risk per Liss et al. CMAJ 1997 and Brown et al. 2017. Latex-fruit syndrome per Wagner & Breiteneder 2002 (PMID 12365131). Diagnostic algorithms per Heese et al. AAAAI guidance and EAACI 2018. WHO/CDC latex avoidance protocols cross-checked.

h1Title: Latex Allergy: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Latex allergy is an IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to proteins in natural rubber latex (NRL) harvested from the Hevea brasiliensis tree. Adult prevalence in the general population is roughly 1-2%, but rises to 10-17% in healthcare workers and 30-72% in children with spina bifida who have had multiple bladder catheterizations and orthopedic procedures. Reactions occur on direct skin or mucosal contact and through inhalation of cornstarch glove powder that carries latex proteins, ranging from contact urticaria within minutes to systemic anaphylaxis. The single most important treatment is strict avoidance of all natural rubber latex products, including in healthcare settings; powder-free, low-protein synthetic gloves have reduced occupational sensitization rates by more than 80% in countries that adopted them as standard. Allergic individuals carry an epinephrine auto-injector and a medical alert card identifying latex allergy as well as the cross-reactive plant foods.

keyStats.prevalence: General population 1-2%; healthcare workers 10-17%; spina bifida children 30-72%; rubber industry workers 10-13%
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio approximately 1.5:1 in healthcare cohorts; equal sex distribution in spina bifida and atopic populations
keyStats.avgAge: Onset peaks in young adulthood for occupational sensitization; pediatric latex allergy is recognized from infancy in spina bifida
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 100-300 million individuals with detectable IgE to natural rubber latex worldwide; far fewer have clinical disease

definition: Latex allergy (ICD-10: T78.40 with sub-codes for type of reaction) is an immediate (type I) IgE-mediated hypersensitivity to one or more of at least 15 allergenic proteins (Hev b 1-15) extracted from the sap of the Hevea brasiliensis tree. Hev b 1, 3, 5, 6.01, 6.02, 7, and 11 are the major clinically relevant allergens; Hev b 5 and Hev b 6.02 dominate in healthcare workers, while Hev b 1 and Hev b 3 are more important in children with spina bifida. Mechanistically, prior sensitization produces specific IgE that binds to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils; re-exposure to latex protein cross-links these receptors, releasing histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes, and other mediators within minutes. Latex allergy is distinct from irritant contact dermatitis (a non-immune chemical irritation) and from type IV (delayed) contact dermatitis caused by rubber accelerators such as thiurams and carbamates. A cross-reactive latex-food syndrome affects 30-50% of latex-allergic individuals because of shared protein homology (profilins, class I chitinases) with avocado, banana, chestnut, kiwi, papaya, and other fresh fruits. Management is led by allergy and immunology specialists in partnership with primary care, surgical, dental, and occupational health teams; the primary intervention is rigorous latex avoidance across home, workplace, and medical environments.

typesClassification.description: True immediate hypersensitivity to latex proteins. Symptoms appear within minutes of skin, mucosal, or inhalational exposure and range from localized urticaria through angioedema and bronchospasm to anaphylaxis. Confirmed by specific IgE testing and skin-prick testing.

typesClassification.type: Type IV (delayed) contact dermatitis to rubber chemicals
typesClassification.description: Cell-mediated reaction to accelerator chemicals (thiurams, carbamates, mercaptobenzothiazoles) added during latex manufacturing rather than to latex protein itself. Presents as eczematous dermatitis 24-72 hours after exposure. Diagnosed by patch testing.

Non-immune mechanical or chemical irritation from frequent handwashing, glove powder, and sweat under occlusive gloves. The most common skin reaction to glove use; does not require allergy testing but predisposes to true latex sensitization.

Cross-reactivity to plant foods sharing homologous proteins with latex. Avocado, banana, chestnut, and kiwi are most often implicated. Affects 30-50% of latex-allergic patients; symptoms range from oral allergy syndrome to anaphylaxis.

Sensitization in healthcare, dental, food service, and rubber industry workers. Powdered gloves are the dominant route of inhalational exposure. Reactions may begin as occupational asthma and progress to anaphylaxis on direct mucosal contact.

primarySymptoms: Contact urticaria — itchy red wheals appearing within 5-30 minutes at sites of latex contact (hands under gloves, lips after dental work, perineum after catheterization).
Angioedema of lips, tongue, eyelids, or larynx after exposure to balloons, gloves, condoms, or dental dam.
Allergic rhinitis with sneezing, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea on inhalation of latex glove powder aerosols.
Asthma symptoms (wheeze, cough, chest tightness, shortness of breath) in latex-sensitized workers, especially in glove-use environments.
Conjunctivitis with itchy watery eyes after direct or aerosol exposure.
Generalized urticaria and pruritus extending beyond the contact site within 15-60 minutes of exposure.
Gastrointestinal symptoms — nausea, abdominal cramps, vomiting, diarrhea — particularly with mucosal exposure during surgical or dental procedures.
Anaphylaxis with hypotension, tachycardia, throat tightness, and loss of consciousness, most often during surgery, dental work, or childbirth.
Oral allergy syndrome with itching and swelling of mouth and throat after eating avocado, banana, chestnut, kiwi, or other cross-reactive foods.
Hand dermatitis with dryness, fissuring, and eczematous changes after repeated glove use (often a combination of irritant, type IV, and type I mechanisms).
earlyWarningSigns: Itchy red hands under latex gloves within 30 minutes of donning
Sneezing, nasal congestion, or watery eyes when entering rooms where powdered latex gloves are in use
Lip swelling or itchy mouth after eating avocado, banana, or chestnut
Recurrent unexplained rashes after dental or gynecological examinations
New wheeze in a healthcare worker that improves on days off
emergencySigns: Sudden hypotension, throat tightness, or stridor during a surgical or dental procedure — assume latex anaphylaxis and remove all latex from the room
Generalized urticaria with wheeze, hoarseness, or vomiting after any latex exposure
Loss of consciousness, severe tachycardia, or cyanosis after balloon contact, catheterization, or condom use
Cardiac arrest of unclear cause during anesthesia in a known or suspected latex-allergic patient
Acute respiratory distress in a healthcare worker after donning latex gloves or entering a powder-contaminated area

causes.cause: Direct skin contact with natural rubber latex
causes.description: Latex gloves are the dominant trigger in healthcare workers, dental staff, food handlers, and laboratory personnel. Repeated occlusive contact damages the skin barrier and increases protein penetration. Other contact sources include balloons, condoms, rubber bands, elastic clothing, and toy balls.

causes.cause: Mucosal exposure during medical or dental procedures
causes.description: Surgical and dental gloves, urinary catheters, enema tips, dental dams, condoms, and tourniquets bring latex protein into contact with mucous membranes, which absorb allergen more rapidly than intact skin. Intraoperative anaphylaxis is the most dangerous presentation.

causes.cause: Inhalation of cornstarch powder carrying latex protein
causes.description: Powdered latex gloves release airborne particles whenever donned or doffed. The powder binds latex protein and disperses it through ventilation systems, exposing every person in a clinic, operating theatre, or laboratory. Removing powder reduces airborne allergen by more than 99%.

causes.cause: Repeated catheterization and surgery in early childhood
causes.description: Children with spina bifida, congenital urinary or anorectal malformations, ventriculo-peritoneal shunts, and those requiring multiple surgeries develop latex sensitization rates as high as 72% because of repeated mucosal exposure during surgery, bladder care, and bowel programs. Latex-safe environments from birth reduce sensitization by 80-95%.

Profilins (Hev b 8), class I chitinases, and patatin-like proteins are conserved across latex and many fruits and vegetables. Initial sensitization can occur via either route; latex sensitization later produces oral allergy syndrome on first exposure to avocado, banana, chestnut, kiwi, papaya, fig, melon, peach, tomato, potato, and bell pepper in many patients.

Atopic dermatitis, asthma, and allergic rhinitis double or triple the risk of developing latex allergy on subsequent occupational exposure. HLA-DR4 and DR7 associations have been described. Family history of atopy further increases susceptibility.

riskFactors.factor: Spina bifida or congenital urogenital malformation
riskFactors.description: 30-72% prevalence in spina bifida cohorts. Repeated surgical and mucosal latex exposures from infancy drive sensitization, although latex-safe pediatric protocols since the 1990s have reduced rates substantially in newly diagnosed children.

10-17% prevalence in healthcare workers, dentists, and laboratory staff in studies before powder-free gloves were standard. Powder-free, low-protein nitrile gloves have driven new sensitization rates below 1% in countries where they are mandated.

riskFactors.factor: Atopy (asthma, allergic rhinitis, eczema, food allergy)
riskFactors.description: Atopic individuals develop latex sensitization at 2-3 times the rate of non-atopic peers when occupationally exposed. Eczema disrupts the skin barrier and accelerates protein penetration.

Each additional surgery or mucosal procedure with latex contact increases sensitization risk. Children with more than three surgeries before age 5 are particularly at risk regardless of underlying diagnosis.
10-13% prevalence in rubber product manufacturing because of prolonged inhalation and dermal exposure. Engineering controls and respiratory protection reduce sensitization but rarely eliminate it.
Allergy to avocado, banana, chestnut, or kiwi can precede or follow latex allergy through shared cross-reactive proteins. Identifying one allergy should prompt screening for the other.
Female-to-male ratio approximately 1.5:1 in occupational studies, partly reflecting female predominance in nursing, dental, and laboratory roles.

affectedDemographics: Healthcare workers 10-17% in studies preceding powder-free glove mandates; under 1% incidence in recent cohorts
Spina bifida children 30-72%; falling toward 10-20% under latex-safe protocols
Rubber industry workers 10-13%; respiratory symptoms in 5-8%
Pediatric atopic cohorts (asthma, eczema, multiple food allergies) 5-10%
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis begins with a structured history of reactions in healthcare, dental, and household settings, including timing relative to exposure, severity, and any history of cross-reactive food reactions. The clinician maps every potential latex contact in the patient's life — gloves, condoms, balloons, toys, elastic clothing, catheters, dental work — and identifies a temporal pattern. Skin-prick testing with standardized natural rubber latex extract is the reference standard in countries where the extract is licensed; sensitivity is 95-99% and specificity 95-100%. Where licensed extract is unavailable (notably the United States), the use-test with a latex glove (finger of a glove dampened and applied to the skin or briefly worn) under controlled conditions is acceptable with full resuscitation equipment available. Serum latex-specific IgE (CAP-FEIA, ImmunoCAP) and component-resolved diagnostics (Hev b 1, 3, 5, 6.01, 6.02, 11) refine the diagnosis and identify true allergens versus cross-reactive carbohydrate determinants. Component testing distinguishes occupational sensitization profiles (Hev b 5, 6.02 dominant) from spina bifida profiles (Hev b 1, 3 dominant). Tryptase measurement within 60-120 minutes of an acute reaction supports anaphylaxis and can be repeated at baseline 24 hours later. Patch testing identifies coexisting type IV contact dermatitis to rubber accelerators. A negative skin and serum test in a patient with a convincing history may warrant a supervised challenge in specialist allergy units.

diagnosticTests.test: Skin-prick test with standardized natural rubber latex extract
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard for IgE-mediated latex allergy in countries where licensed extract is available (Europe, Canada, Australia)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Allergy clinic procedure of 30-45 minutes. A drop of extract is placed on the forearm and pricked with a lancet; wheal of 3 mm or greater after 15 minutes confirms sensitization. Emergency drugs are immediately available.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies allergen-specific IgE in blood; sensitivity 70-90%, specificity 80-95%
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return within 5-10 days. Particularly useful when skin testing is contraindicated (severe dermatitis, recent anaphylaxis, antihistamine use).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies specific allergens, distinguishes true allergy from cross-reactive carbohydrate determinants, and refines risk profile (spina bifida versus occupational pattern)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on the same serum sample as latex-specific IgE; usually adds 1-2 weeks to reporting time.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms clinical reactivity when skin and serum tests are inconclusive or unavailable
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed in a supervised allergy setting with resuscitation equipment. A latex glove is moistened and applied to a small area of skin or briefly worn; reactions are monitored for 30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Patch testing with rubber accelerator panel
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies coexisting type IV contact dermatitis to thiurams, carbamates, and mercaptobenzothiazoles
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patches are applied to the back for 48 hours, then read at 48 and 96 hours. Mild redness is common; persistent red papular reactions confirm sensitization.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum tryptase within 60-120 minutes of an acute reaction
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports diagnosis of anaphylaxis; comparison with baseline level 24 hours later increases specificity
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw timed to the reaction. Baseline tryptase repeated 24 hours after the reaction has resolved.

treatmentOverview: Treatment combines immediate management of acute reactions, strict latex avoidance, and education for emergency situations. Acute reactions are graded by severity: localized contact urticaria is managed with topical or oral antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine, fexofenadine), while anaphylaxis is treated with intramuscular epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg in adults or 0.01 mg/kg (maximum 0.3 mg) in children, repeated every 5-15 minutes as needed, with supplemental oxygen, intravenous fluids, and supine positioning. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts to, not substitutes for, epinephrine. After stabilization, patients are observed for biphasic reactions for 4-6 hours and prescribed two epinephrine auto-injectors with explicit usage instruction. Strict avoidance is the cornerstone of long-term management: replacement of latex gloves with synthetic alternatives (nitrile, neoprene, vinyl), avoidance of balloons, condoms, dental dam, latex-containing elastic clothing, and pre-procedure notification of dental, surgical, and obstetric teams to organize latex-safe environments. Healthcare workers may require occupational accommodations or, if reactions are severe, redeployment to a low-latex environment. There is currently no licensed allergen-specific immunotherapy for latex allergy outside of clinical trials, although sublingual immunotherapy with hypoallergenic Hev b 5 fragments has shown promise in early-phase studies. Patients with documented latex-food syndrome receive dietary counseling and may require an emergency action plan for cross-reactive food reactions. Medical alert bracelets and an allergy passport prevent inadvertent latex exposure during anesthesia and emergency care.

medicalTreatments.name: Intramuscular epinephrine (adrenaline) 0.3-0.5 mg adult, 0.01 mg/kg child
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for any latex anaphylaxis. Given via the mid-outer thigh; can be repeated every 5-15 minutes. Two auto-injectors are prescribed for all patients with prior systemic reactions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution of symptoms in over 90% of anaphylactic reactions when given within 5 minutes of onset

medicalTreatments.name: Second-generation oral antihistamines (cetirizine 10 mg, fexofenadine 180 mg, loratadine 10 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive treatment for urticaria, rhinitis, and conjunctivitis. Pre-treatment before potential latex exposure (e.g., dental visits) is sometimes used but does not substitute for avoidance.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom reduction in 60-80% of mild cutaneous and respiratory reactions

medicalTreatments.name: Inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators
medicalTreatments.description: Standard management for latex-induced occupational asthma. Inhaled corticosteroids (fluticasone, budesonide, beclomethasone) are first-line; short-acting beta-agonists (salbutamol) for rescue.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom control in 70-85% of patients combined with workplace avoidance

medicalTreatments.name: Systemic corticosteroids (prednisolone 40-60 mg for 3-5 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for severe or biphasic anaphylactic reactions and for severe systemic urticaria/angioedema not responding to antihistamines. Onset of action delays effect by hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduction in biphasic reaction risk; symptom resolution in severe reactions over 24-72 hours

medicalTreatments.name: Sublingual immunotherapy with latex extract (investigational)
medicalTreatments.description: Standardized latex sublingual immunotherapy has shown reduction in skin reactivity and quality-of-life improvement in randomized trials of healthcare workers. Not yet licensed in most countries.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 50-60% reduction in symptoms in early-phase European trials; long-term efficacy data still maturing

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-procedure latex-safe surgical and dental protocols
alternativeTreatments.description: Substitution of nitrile or synthetic alternatives for every latex item (gloves, drains, catheters, anesthesia masks, blood pressure cuffs) and operating in a latex-cleaned theatre as the first case of the day. Reduces intraoperative reactions by over 95%.

alternativeTreatments.name: Occupational health risk reduction
alternativeTreatments.description: Workplace mandate of powder-free, low-protein synthetic gloves, regular environmental monitoring, and education for all staff. Implementation has reduced sensitization to under 1% in many European and North American hospitals.

whySeeSpecialist: Latex allergy ranges from mild contact urticaria to fatal intraoperative anaphylaxis, and the diagnosis affects every future medical encounter. An allergy and immunology specialist confirms the diagnosis with appropriate testing, identifies cross-reactive foods, prescribes epinephrine auto-injectors, and coordinates latex-safe protocols with anesthesia, surgery, dental, and obstetric teams.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose an allergist with experience in occupational allergy and access to standardized latex extract, serum specific IgE, and component-resolved diagnostics. Patients with anaphylaxis should be seen at a center with a written latex-safe protocol covering inpatient and outpatient care.
hospitalCriteria: Written latex-safe protocol covering operating theatres, dental services, emergency department, and inpatient wards
Powder-free, low-protein synthetic gloves available throughout the institution
Pre-procedure allergy review for elective surgical and dental patients
keyFacilities: Pediatric spina bifida and neurosurgery units
preventionStrategies: Use powder-free, low-protein synthetic gloves in every healthcare and dental setting.
Notify dentists, surgeons, anesthetists, gynecologists, and obstetricians of latex allergy at every visit; carry a latex allergy alert card.
Operate latex-safe environments from birth in children with spina bifida or expected multiple surgeries.
Replace household and recreational latex items (gloves, balloons, condoms, rubber bands, dental dam) with synthetic alternatives.
Read product labels and food labels carefully to avoid latex-containing items and cross-reactive foods.
Carry an epinephrine auto-injector and an allergy action plan if any systemic reaction has occurred.
lifestyleModifications: Wear a medical alert bracelet or carry an allergy card identifying latex allergy and cross-reactive foods.
Inform employers, schools, and childcare providers in writing; check the latex content of cleaning gloves, art supplies, and physical education equipment.
Use only synthetic condoms (polyurethane, polyisoprene) or non-latex barrier contraception.
Inspect every new product for latex content; common hidden sources include rubber bands, balloons, elastic in clothing, and toy balls.
Keep two in-date epinephrine auto-injectors with you at all times if prescribed.
Plan dental and medical appointments as the first appointment of the day in a latex-cleaned room.

recommended: Maintain a balanced diet without unnecessary food restriction if no cross-reactive food allergies are confirmed
Substitute tropical fruits with non-reactive alternatives (apple, pear, berries) if latex-food syndrome is present
Cooked vegetables and fruits often tolerated better than raw in oral allergy syndrome, but should be confirmed with the specialist
Allergen-free packaged foods with clear labelling for severe cross-reactive reactions
avoid: Avocado, banana, chestnut, and kiwi if cross-reactivity is documented (30-50% of latex-allergic patients)
Papaya, fig, melon, peach, tomato, potato, bell pepper, and passion fruit in highly cross-reactive patients
Foods without ingredient labels in restaurants until cross-reactivity is clarified
Untested new tropical fruits without prior allergy advice

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is safe and encouraged. Avoid balloons in birthday or fitness settings, use synthetic exercise mats and resistance bands, and inspect gym equipment for latex grips. Carry epinephrine in case of exercise-induced reactions to a recently eaten cross-reactive food.
dailyManagement: Carry two in-date epinephrine auto-injectors and an allergy action plan at all times.
Check every new product, glove, and elastic item for latex content before use.
Inform every healthcare provider of latex allergy at every encounter, including pharmacies and laboratories.
Inspect work and home environments for latex contamination after any reaction.
Renew epinephrine auto-injectors before expiry and after each use.
Schedule annual allergy review to update the allergy action plan and confirm cross-reactivity profile.
prognosis: With strict avoidance, latex allergy is well controlled in the great majority of patients. Healthcare workers who switch to synthetic gloves and avoid powder-contaminated environments achieve symptom remission in 80-95% within 6-12 months, although established latex-induced asthma may persist long-term. The risk of fatal anaphylaxis is virtually eliminated when latex-safe protocols are in place for surgery, dental work, and obstetric care. Children with spina bifida born into latex-safe environments now have sensitization rates of 10-20% versus 30-72% in older cohorts. Sensitization typically persists for years; specific IgE may slowly decline but rarely disappears entirely. Occupational asthma diagnosed and avoided within the first year has a 70-85% probability of remission, falling below 30% once disease has been established for over 5 years.
recoveryTimeline: Acute reactions resolve within minutes to hours of treatment. Occupational symptoms in healthcare workers improve over 4-12 weeks of latex-safe glove use. Latex-specific IgE titers fall slowly over 5-10 years of strict avoidance, and skin test reactivity may diminish but rarely returns to negative without persistent sensitization.
complications: Anaphylaxis during surgery, dental procedures, or childbirth — the most feared complication; preventable with latex-safe protocols.
Occupational asthma in healthcare and rubber industry workers — may persist after exposure ceases and limit career options.
Chronic urticaria and dermatitis with skin barrier dysfunction and secondary bacterial infection.
Latex-food syndrome with anaphylaxis to avocado, banana, chestnut, or kiwi — requires dietary counseling and emergency action plans.
Treatment delays during emergencies when latex exposure is not recognized; medical alert identification is critical.
Psychological burden of constant vigilance and limited career options, especially in healthcare workers; structured occupational health support is recommended.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Allergy, Asthma and Immunology (AAAAI) — Latex Allergy
supportResources.description: Patient education on latex allergy, cross-reactive foods, and finding a latex-allergy specialist.

supportResources.name: American Latex Allergy Association
supportResources.description: Patient-focused organization providing safe-product lists, latex-free resource directories, and community support.

supportResources.name: European Academy of Allergy and Clinical Immunology (EAACI)
supportResources.description: Professional society resources including position papers on natural rubber latex allergy.

supportResources.name: Spina Bifida Association — Latex Allergy
supportResources.description: Patient and family guidance on managing latex allergy in children with spina bifida.

confusedWithConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Type IV allergic contact dermatitis to rubber accelerators (thiurams, carbamates) appears 24-72 hours after exposure as eczema and is diagnosed by patch testing. Type I latex allergy appears within minutes as urticaria, angioedema, or anaphylaxis and is diagnosed by skin-prick or serum-IgE testing. The two can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaphylaxis is the systemic syndrome that can result from many triggers including latex, food, drugs, and insect stings. Latex allergy is one specific cause of anaphylaxis identified by history and confirmed by allergy testing. Treatment is the same; identifying the trigger informs prevention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eczema (Atopic Dermatitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Atopic eczema is chronic, often starts in childhood, and flares in characteristic distribution (flexures in children, hands and face in adults). Latex contact urticaria is acute, occurs within minutes of exposure, and confined initially to contact sites. Atopic eczema predisposes to but does not equal latex allergy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Food Allergy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Food allergy and latex allergy can coexist through latex-food syndrome (cross-reactivity to avocado, banana, chestnut, kiwi). Food allergy is triggered by ingestion; latex allergy by skin, mucosal, or inhalational contact. Components and history distinguish primary from cross-reactive sensitization.

relatedConditions.name: Anaphylaxis
relatedConditions.relevance: Most severe manifestation of latex allergy; shared treatment and emergency action plan.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Latex-induced occupational asthma is a major form of disease and shares management pathways.

relatedConditions.name: Food Allergy
relatedConditions.relevance: 30-50% of latex-allergic individuals have cross-reactive food allergies through latex-food syndrome.

relatedConditions.name: Eczema
relatedConditions.relevance: Atopic dermatitis disrupts the skin barrier and increases the risk of subsequent latex sensitization, particularly in healthcare workers.

relatedConditions.name: Allergic Contact Dermatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Often coexists with latex allergy and reflects sensitization to rubber accelerators rather than the latex protein itself.

faqs.question: What is latex allergy in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Latex allergy is an immune reaction to proteins in natural rubber from the rubber tree. The reaction can cause itchy skin, hives, swelling, asthma, or anaphylaxis within minutes of touching, breathing, or being internally exposed to latex products such as gloves, balloons, and condoms.

faqs.question: What causes latex allergy?
faqs.answer: Repeated exposure to proteins in natural rubber latex sensitizes the immune system to make latex-specific IgE antibodies. Highest-risk groups are children with spina bifida (multiple surgeries), healthcare and dental workers, rubber industry employees, and people with atopic disease.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of latex allergy?
faqs.answer: Symptoms appear within minutes and range from itchy red hives at the contact site to swelling of the lips and tongue, sneezing, runny nose, wheezing, asthma, low blood pressure, and anaphylaxis. Symptoms can occur from skin contact, mucosal contact, or breathing in glove powder.

faqs.question: How is latex allergy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines a history of reactions, skin-prick testing with standardized latex extract (where available), and serum latex-specific IgE testing. Component-resolved diagnostics identify specific allergens (Hev b 1, 3, 5, 6.02). Use-tests in supervised settings confirm diagnosis when other tests are inconclusive.

faqs.question: Can latex allergy cause anaphylaxis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intraoperative anaphylaxis is the most dangerous presentation, particularly during surgery, dental work, or obstetric procedures. Latex anaphylaxis is treated with intramuscular epinephrine and immediate removal of all latex from the environment.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for latex allergy?
faqs.answer: The cornerstone is strict avoidance of all natural rubber latex products. Acute reactions are treated with intramuscular epinephrine for anaphylaxis and antihistamines for milder symptoms. Two epinephrine auto-injectors are prescribed for patients with any history of systemic reaction.

faqs.question: Is there a cure for latex allergy?
faqs.answer: There is no licensed cure. Sublingual immunotherapy with latex extract is investigational and has shown 50-60% symptom reduction in early trials. Strict avoidance remains the primary management. Latex-specific IgE may slowly decline over years but rarely disappears entirely.

faqs.question: Why are healthcare workers at higher risk?
faqs.answer: Repeated wearing and removal of powdered latex gloves exposes healthcare workers to high doses of latex protein through skin and inhaled powder. Pre-2000 prevalence in healthcare workers was 10-17%. Powder-free, low-protein gloves have reduced new sensitization to under 1%.

faqs.question: What foods cross-react with latex?
faqs.answer: About 30-50% of latex-allergic individuals react to avocado, banana, chestnut, and kiwi, with smaller numbers reacting to papaya, fig, melon, peach, tomato, potato, and bell pepper. These cross-reactions arise from shared proteins (profilins, class I chitinases) between latex and plants.

faqs.question: Can I have surgery if I have latex allergy?
faqs.answer: Yes, with appropriate planning. Latex-safe operating theatres use synthetic gloves, drains, catheters, and anesthesia equipment, and schedule the patient as the first case of the day in a latex-cleaned room. Inform the surgical and anesthesia teams at every pre-operative visit.

faqs.question: Is latex allergy genetic?
faqs.answer: There is no single gene, but susceptibility runs in atopic families. HLA-DR4 and DR7 associations have been described, and family history of asthma, eczema, or allergic rhinitis increases risk by 2-3 fold. Most cases reflect environmental sensitization in genetically susceptible individuals.

faqs.question: Can children outgrow latex allergy?
faqs.answer: Some children with spina bifida and low-level sensitization may lose skin test reactivity over many years of strict avoidance, but most retain detectable IgE for life. Children with documented anaphylaxis should be considered allergic indefinitely and carry epinephrine auto-injectors.

faqs.question: What products contain latex?
faqs.answer: Common latex items include gloves, balloons, condoms, dental dam, rubber bands, elastic clothing, tourniquets, urinary catheters, mouse pads, pencil erasers, rubber toys, and some adhesives. Manufacturers must label medical products, but many household items are unlabeled — read packaging carefully.

faqs.question: Are vinyl or nitrile gloves safe?
faqs.answer: Yes. Vinyl, nitrile, and polychloroprene (neoprene) gloves contain no natural rubber latex protein and are safe for latex-allergic individuals. Powder-free, low-protein synthetic gloves are now standard in most healthcare and food service settings.

faqs.question: What is latex-fruit syndrome?
faqs.answer: Latex-fruit syndrome is cross-reactivity between latex proteins and homologous proteins in fresh fruits and vegetables, particularly avocado, banana, chestnut, and kiwi. It affects 30-50% of latex-allergic individuals and can cause oral allergy syndrome through to anaphylaxis.

faqs.question: How do I tell my dentist or doctor about latex allergy?
faqs.answer: Notify every dental, medical, and surgical provider at booking and again at the appointment. Carry a written allergy card or wear a medical alert bracelet, and request that your records flag latex allergy. Ask whether the practice has a written latex-safe protocol.

faqs.question: Do condoms have to be latex?
faqs.answer: No. Polyurethane and polyisoprene condoms are widely available and provide similar protection against pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections. Natural skin (lambskin) condoms are not recommended because they do not prevent infection transmission.

faqs.question: Can latex allergy be life threatening?
faqs.answer: Yes. Anaphylaxis from latex exposure during surgery, childbirth, dental work, or condom use can be fatal. Risk is virtually eliminated when latex-safe protocols are implemented and patients carry epinephrine. Medical alert bracelets and allergy cards are essential.

faqs.question: Do I need an epinephrine auto-injector?
faqs.answer: Patients with any history of systemic reaction (more than localized contact urticaria) should carry two in-date epinephrine auto-injectors at all times. Children should also have auto-injectors at school. The auto-injector is given intramuscularly into the mid-outer thigh.

faqs.question: Can latex allergy improve over time?
faqs.answer: With strict avoidance, occupational symptoms in healthcare workers improve over 4-12 weeks and 80-95% achieve symptom remission within 6-12 months. Established latex-induced asthma may persist long-term, especially when diagnosis is delayed beyond 5 years.

faqs.question: Why are spina bifida children at high risk?
faqs.answer: Children with spina bifida have multiple surgeries, bladder catheterizations, and bowel procedures from infancy that bring mucosal surfaces into prolonged contact with latex. Up to 72% develop sensitization in older cohorts; latex-safe protocols from birth reduce this rate by 80-95%.

metaTitle: Latex Allergy: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Latex allergy is an IgE reaction to natural rubber. Symptoms, cross-reactive foods, anaphylaxis treatment, and avoidance, medically reviewed.

sources.title: Bousquet J, Flahault A, Vandenplas O, et al. 2006. Natural rubber latex allergy among health care workers: a systematic review of the evidence. J Allergy Clin Immunol 118(2):447-454.

Cabanes N, Igea JM, de la Hoz B, et al. 2012. Latex allergy: position paper. J Investig Allergol Clin Immunol 22(5):313-330.

Allmers H, Schmengler J, John SM. 2004. Decreasing incidence of occupational contact urticaria caused by natural rubber latex allergy in German health care workers. J Allergy Clin Immunol 114(2):347-351.

Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. NIOSH Alert: preventing allergic reactions to natural rubber latex in the workplace. CDC, Cincinnati.
Spina Bifida Association. Latex in the hospital environment. Washington DC.
